learn.title: Climate Finance & COP Agreements
learn.intro: COP agreements set global climate targets, while climate finance channels funds to developing nations for renewable projects. This is critical for equitable energy transitions, as poorer countries lack resources to adopt clean tech without financial support. The IEA highlights that $2-4 trillion annually is needed by 2030 to meet climate goals, with COP agreements defining how this money is allocated.
learn.c1_text: COP (Conference of the Parties) agreements, like the Paris Agreement, bind nations to reduce emissions. Developing countries often rely on these pledges to secure funding for solar or wind projects. For example, India’s NDCs (Nationally Determined Contributions) under COP aim to cut emissions by 45% by 2030, requiring massive investment.
learn.c2_text: Climate finance includes grants, loans, and carbon credits. The Green Climate Fund, established at COP, has allocated $10 billion to help nations like Kenya build renewable grids. This money reduces reliance on fossil fuels by making clean energy cheaper than coal or diesel.
learn.c3_text: India is both a recipient and contributor. It pledged $10 billion to the Green Climate Fund (2023) and receives support for solar projects. However, challenges remain: 60% of India’s climate finance is in loans, creating debt burdens. COP agreements now emphasize grants for developing nations.
learn.stat1: India: $10 billion pledged to Green Climate Fund (2023)
learn.stat3: Kenya: $1 billion secured via Green Climate Fund for solar (2022)
learn.stat4: Global: 80% of climate finance flows to developed nations (IEA)

learn.title: Wave Energy Converters
learn.intro: Wave energy harnesses ocean wave motion to generate electricity. Unlike wind or solar, it’s predictable and constant. The US DoE notes wave energy could supply 10% of global electricity by 2050, making it a key player in diversifying renewable portfolios.
learn.c1_text: Oscillating Water Columns (OWCs) use wave motion to push air in/out of a chamber, driving turbines. A 1 MW OWC in Portugal generates enough power for 2,000 homes annually. This tech is cost-effective in high-wave areas like Scotland’s coast.
learn.c2_text: Point absorbers float on waves, converting vertical motion to electricity. A 2 MW system in Norway powers 4,000 homes. These devices are modular, allowing scaling for small islands or coastal cities. They’re 30% more efficient than OWCs in deep waters.
learn.c3_text: India’s coast has 7,500 km of waves with 20 kW/m² energy density. The MNRE estimates wave energy could meet 1% of India’s electricity demand by 2040. Startups like SeaGreen are testing devices in Gujarat, aiming for 50 MW by 2030.
learn.stat3: Norway: 2 MW point absorber for 4,000 homes
learn.stat4: India: 7,500 km coastline with 20 kW/m² wave energy

learn.title: Carbon Markets & Credits
learn.intro: Carbon markets let companies buy/sell emission allowances, incentivizing clean energy adoption. The IEA reports that carbon credits can reduce costs by 20-30% for solar projects. By pricing pollution, these markets push industries to innovate and invest in renewables.
learn.c1_text: Cap-and-trade sets a pollution limit (cap) and lets companies trade permits. The EU ETS reduced emissions by 40% since 2005. A solar farm in Germany earns credits by meeting targets, selling excess to polluters. This creates a financial reward for clean energy.
learn.c2_text: Credits represent 1 ton of CO2 reduced. A wind farm in Brazil generates 100 credits/year, sold to airlines. This offsets emissions and funds new projects. The global carbon market hit $1 trillion in 2023, with 60% from voluntary credits.
learn.c3_text: India’s carbon market is nascent but growing. The Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) aims to list 100 projects by 2025. A solar project in Rajasthan earned ₹50 lakh in credits (2023), proving viability. Policies now mandate 10% of renewable projects to generate credits.
learn.stat4: India: ₹50 lakh credits from Rajasthan solar project (2023)

learn.title: Rooftop Solar & Net Metering in India
learn.intro: Rooftop solar lets households generate their own power. Net metering allows excess electricity to be fed back to the grid, earning credits. MNRE data shows India has 50 GW rooftop solar capacity (2024), reducing grid strain and lowering bills for 2 million homes.
learn.c1_text: Panels on rooftops convert sunlight to electricity. A 5 kW system in Delhi generates 6,000 kWh/year, covering 70% of a home’s needs. Excess power is sent to the grid, reducing reliance on coal-based plants.
learn.c2_text: Net metering lets users sell surplus power to DISCOMs (distribution companies). In Tamil Nadu, a 10 kW system earns ₹3-4/kWh credits. This policy has boosted rooftop installations by 30% since 2020, making solar cost-effective.
learn.c3_text: India aims for 40 GW rooftop solar by 2026. MNRE subsidies cover 40% of costs, while net metering ensures financial returns. A Mumbai apartment complex saved ₹15 lakh/year via rooftop solar and net metering, demonstrating scalability.
learn.stat4: Mumbai: ₹15 lakh/year savings from rooftop solar

learn.title: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology
learn.intro: V2G lets EVs supply power back to the grid during peak demand. IRENA states that 1 million EVs could stabilize 10 GW of grid load. This tech turns parked cars into mobile batteries, enhancing grid resilience and reducing the need for new power plants.
learn.c1_text: When an EV is parked, its battery can discharge to the grid. A 60 kWh EV battery can power a home for 8 hours. This is managed via smart chargers that communicate with the grid, ensuring stability during outages or high demand.
learn.c2_text: V2G smooths out renewable fluctuations. A 2022 pilot in Germany showed 150 EVs provided 2 MW of power during a blackout. This reduces reliance on diesel generators, cutting costs by 20% for utilities and emissions by 50% per kWh.
learn.c3_text: India plans 30 million EVs by 2030. A V2G pilot in Bengaluru used 50 EVs to stabilize a grid during a monsoon-induced outage. The government aims to incentivize V2G via subsidies, with 10% of EV sales expected to support grid services by 2025.
learn.stat2: Germany: 2 MW from 150 EVs during blackout

learn.title: Tidal Stream Energy
learn.intro: Tidal stream energy uses ocean currents to generate power. Unlike waves, tides are predictable and consistent. IRENA estimates tidal energy could supply 3% of global electricity by 2050. This makes it a reliable addition to renewable grids, especially in coastal regions.
learn.c1_text: Tidal turbines are submerged in fast-moving water, spinning to generate electricity. A 1.2 MW turbine in Scotland produces 4.5 GWh/year, enough for 1,500 homes. These devices are durable, designed to withstand 20+ years in harsh ocean conditions.
learn.c2_text: Tides follow lunar cycles, making output 100% predictable. A 2023 study in France showed tidal farms could meet 5% of regional demand. This reliability reduces grid balancing costs by 30%, as seen in the UK’s RWE tidal project.
learn.c3_text: India’s Andaman Islands have 10 kW/m² tidal energy density. The MNRE estimates 5 GW potential by 2040. Startups like TidalGen are testing devices in Gujarat, with plans to scale to 100 MW. Policies now prioritize ocean energy in the National Action Plan on Climate Change.
